title: 2025/09/25 - JU [Part 3]
description: Thursday 25 September 2025 | Justice Committee Electoral Amendment Bill Submitters: Debbie Port Te Runanga o Ngati Ruanui Trust - Graham Young, Policy Manager E tu - Karena Brown, Lead Strategic Researcher Charlotte Evans Danya Yang Ethan Kehoe Andrew Riddell Simon O'Neill The Law Association of New Zealand - Geoffrey Beresford, Partner, Beresford Law Taurikura NZ - Ana Casey-Cox, - Maxine van Oosten,
descriptionSummary: Electoral Amendment Bill. Debbie Port. Te Runanga o Ngati Ruanui Trust

chunks.text: Paul, sorry, sorry for that delay. That's right. Okay. So I live in social housing and it's one thing to try and get people engaged in local body elections. They're gonna be massively infected affected by these changes. Like a family member can actually witness it. Well, if you don't live with it, you want, you've actually gotta go and find a complete stranger. So it's not as easy as some people think it can be quite hard, especially with social housing and, you know, you just don't know what you're meant to do and stuff, so, okay. That is something you guys need to consider. Yeah. Aaron, I'm glad. Thank you for bringing it to our attention. Yeah. Alright, um, now we have got a number of people, um, and Nui trustees, please.

Hello. So now apologize for being late. Uh, you probably heard we had some technical issue. Um, so, but we're eagerly looking forward to your submission. Yes, thank, thank you Mr. Chair. Um, I sat through the technical issues, so it was interesting, so no one could hear me, but everybody was talking, so it was great.

So look, Nati RI welcomes the opportunity to, um, present our submission on the electoral amendment bill, making amendments to the Electoral Act 1993. Nati Ri as always, always endorses any ways to improve the timeliness and efficiency of electoral administration and changes that strengthen the integrity of electoral law.

However, despite the intention of the Bill Nati, URI has serious concerns relating to key aspects of the bill, including removing the right to enroll in the 13 days prior to the election, including the right to enroll on the day itself, um, the ban on voting for people in prison, especially within that three year period, and setting barriers around food and drink and entertainment within a hundred meters of the voting place.

And we also raised the issue of raising, um, uh, the threshold of anonymous donations. We also advocate, advocate for the inclusion of lowering the voting age to 16 following recent international examples and publicly policy statements and positions now being expressed around the world, perhaps unprecedented.

We are fearful and expressed our concerns in respect of the government's lack of attention to the recommendations arising from the Ministry of Justice and the government's attorney general Judith Collins, who had advised that the bill would be in breach of the Bill of Rights. Section 12. When the Attorney General is ignored in respect of the Bill of Rights, the guardrails of democracy start to be dismantled.

We ask that you pause and you think about this. In a world that is currently surrounded by political gestures, this is a time when we should stand strong and uphold our Bill of Rights more strongly than ever before. Overall, Nati advocates for changes that promote the right to vote rather than the strict the rights of a community to exercise one of the most sacred actions of our democracy, which is that right to vote.

The Crown is responsible for upholding obligations under ti, ensuring manano our democratic rights are upheld the right to vote and contest free, fair, and regular elections must remain at the center of any election process. We note the comments from the 2023 Independent electoral review.

The Crown must redress past breaches, actively protect Maori electoral rights, and provide equitable opportunities for Maori to take part in elections. Decades of systematic breaches by the Crown have resulted in consistently lower rates of Maori voter engagement and participation.

The Crown must do better, not our words, but somebody else's. We see no explicit move to uphold the rights expressed above. Um, so there's, there's nothing in this bill that actually improves the position. In fact, it's likely to do the reverse for us. Nati Nu calls out the actions that are undermining our ability to particularly participate in the forthcoming and general election.

In 2026, the right to vote up and on the day of election ensures full participation in the electoral process. The right ensures fair and adjust society, and also ensure the crowns upholds the ability of electoral rights. Removing this right is a significant and backwards step.

The number who could be disenfranchised will be substantial within changes being proposed. The electoral commission report on the election per past election noted that over 110,000 enrollments occurred on the day of the election. Why would you, as a select committee be recommending to parliament that you disenfranchise this number of citizens?

The Attorney General's report specifically demonstrates that this change will further disen, disenfranchise Maori Asian Pacifica and youth voters. We also know that the most affected by this change will be Maori Pacifica and Ranga Tahi and whanau who often move frequently rent.

And those that are transient, these are the hardest to reach through education campaigns and the like. That means we can predict that there will be a substantial number of people not properly enrolled when voting commences, who will lose the right to vote, um, and their vote will not be counted.

Maori Pacific already have already groups with lower participation rates. They need all our support to participate in an election process. The bill does nothing to enhance this position. While there may be some administrative benefits, and I heard the previous discussion, I think, um, in terms of turnaround of results, reducing eligibility for timeframes, processes and efficiency cannot trump voting rights.

The approach actually defies logic in our view and a decent argument in an open and decent society. At the end of the debate, and I know you will have heard a lot, and I know you've heard a lot of legal submissions as well. This bill does one simple thing. It will disenfranchise over 100,000 people from potentially voting next year.

This bill also extends the ban vote voting ban to all people in prison. Mm-hmm. Nty considers that again, once these proposed changes, um, will breach the uh, bill of rights and tar rights, we say this drawing upon finding established by the high court, the Supreme Court, and the Waitangi Tribunal Maori will be disproportionately affected by this bill being overrepresented in prison populations.

This will exclude more of our population from participating in democratic processes and further alienating them from community and civic responsibility. A great example previously was put to you, if someone was in prison for one month, why would we wish to do that? If we want to reconnect those in prison with society and provide a plan for reintegration, we need to retain their right to participate in democracy.

Disenfranchise should not form the part of someone's punishment. The bill proposes to prohibit the provision of food and drink entertainment within 100 meters of polling places. Nati Rooney considers the provision of Kai can be positive means to further engage with the community and participate in voting.

Voting should be accessible, not restrictive. Barriers often ignore tikanga and reduce inclusion. For Maori, the ability to gather share kai and support whanau to vote together is part of Tonga and Mana banging the banning. This is culturally insensitive.

Removing these supports risk further reducing voter turnout. The proposed proposed provision really just looks mean-spirited if the truth be told without any evidence of issues and concerns. Overall, briefly, we note the change in donation threshold for reporting party donors and adjustments.

And we recognize this is minor, however, to account to account for inflation. But this is, this is a significant change and it does potentially send the wrong signal about openness and transparency. Raising the threshold for anonymous donations reduces transparency because our whanau already struggle to see fairness in the pol in politics and weakening accountability risks increasing mistrust in the system.

Finally, we touch on our ranu tahi, our already disengaged from politics. If we want succession and true future leadership, we need more pathways for Orang Tahi to participate, not fewer. The independent electoral review in 2023, general election recommended lowering the voting age.

Voting at 16 means a higher turnout for elections. Overall participation is critical for our democracy, for both central and local government. Nati Rooney therefore recommends lowering the voting age to 16 and evidence supports this change. 16 year olds are just capable of making informed decisions as their counterparts who are 18 years old.

Lowering the voting age could also improve improved participation based on emerging research from other countries. And we consider allowing the routing age will be consistent with engaging with a group, a group that often deal feels disenfranchised. We thank the committee for listening to our submission today. Yep. Now Mihi noi.

That's the basic thing. As a, as a person who's a New Zealand citizen, is the right to be able to vote to decide on who is gonna be your future and stopping them for no reason other than the factors that it's gonna be an administrative process to make it quicker to count there. As the Crown Law said, there were other ways you could mitigate that. We could actually change the rules so that actually when a special vote arrives on the day, on day, it arrives.

It can be open, it can be verified, it can be checked just like a normal vet. And if they, they are, and they've been enrolled. And so it would reduce the amount of vote special votes you'd have to have on the day because you'd actually be doing it every day that they enroll.

And I think that's a, a, a much better way of doing it then it's just saying, no, you can't enroll on the stay, you've lost your right to vote because all we're gonna do is disenfranchise people and create a generation of, of, um, voters who will be really frustrated with the system and will actually end up getting more unenrolled non-voters because they'll say, what's the point?

I wanted to vote and I wasn't able to. And I really am concerned that we are gonna stop people wanting to vote. And people really do choose to, um, take some a while to tre entrench changes to the system and they won't realize they can't. Why do we, why do you say it's disenfranchising when what it's doing is holding up the whole country at the back end of it when we're actually trying to streamline the process for the majority of people? Well, I think you can streamline the process without actually stopping people with the right to vote.

I mean, yes, you're saying, um, yes they can catch a bus to go to work, but that bus timetable has been set for absolutely ages. And often when bus timetables change, people miss buses and they get, and they miss work and they don't get paid. And the same thing's gonna happen with the voting. People have actually, they have actually had, since 1993, they've had the ability to vote up till the day before election day.

The reality is it's a lot harder these days. 'cause see, we haven't been able to get across to them the, that you can actually enroll on election day now. And that's been two elections. So what happens is, first time we have an election and that says you actually can't roll for 13 days.

Bang, you're gonna so many people who aren't, who aren't gonna pick up what that means to them until they go in and try to vote. I mean, if you're gonna say you're gonna do it, you know, we're gonna do it in two years, you know, two elections time, and we're gonna slowly bring it in, you know, so that next, next time, you know, they we're gonna, for the first election, we're gonna cut out the elect, you know, polling day one.

'cause that was one we didn't like. We'll turn back to that one. Yes. But I think just banging it in, people aren't gonna pick up the message and then they're gonna get disenfranchised. Okay. So so three to three to six months isn't long enough, but six years is, is what you're suggesting. What I'm saying is it's taken six years so far and we still haven't got everyone yet. Understanding Polling day was able to be enrolled.

Thank you. What I'm saying is, yeah. Hey, thank you. Um, thank you Ms. Brown. Um, good submission and you've done 'em before, so that's very good. Hey, thank you for joining us this afternoon. Thank you. Right now we've got Charlotte Evans by phone. Is that right? Ms. Evans? Have you, uh, if you're available, uh, feel free.

Uh, hello? Hello? Sorry, it was asking to unmute Charlotte Evans. Yeah, can you hear me? Ah, right, thanks. Yeah, figured it out. Um, yeah, should I start? Yes, please. Okay. So, um, to start with, I'd like to refer back to a couple of points from my written submission, just briefly.

So the proposed changes to enrollment have been presented as an update to the law, but far from an update, they're really a reversion to an older and, and frankly, worse approach, taking us back to the eighties. Um, another particular concern I've had is that, um, kicking people off the unpublished role when they're sentenced to prison is outright dangerous since it may be very difficult for 'em to get back onto it afterwards.

And the bill would do that since those people would have to freshly enroll and reapply for the unpublished role, um, upon leaving prison, which should be a difficult circumstance to do that. And so it could be exposing them to physical risk, having their address out there again, um, by violating prisoners' right to vote.

Uh, the bill would also create a first incentive for governments to criminalize people who oppose them. Uh, in fact, we already see the risk of this in the, uh, the summary offenses, the demonstrations near residential premises amendment bill, which, uh, I believe is also before this committee introduced less than a month after the bill we're discussing here, um, which would criminalize protest outside politicians' homes.

And that provides for a three month prison sentence. So the essence of that bill should be a major red flag for this bill. And then this bill contains a one two punch of automatic enrollment updates and then enclosing enrollments before voting begins. Uh, there's a particularly dangerous combination that could see people disenfranchised in the case that their enrollment details have been incorrectly altered without their knowledge.

And then this is only discovered when they go to vote. They find that they're not on the electoral role or they're on the role in, in some other district. I'm not be sure if they'd be able to find that out at that point. Um, and, uh, yeah, by that point it would be too late to do anything about it because enrollments would be closed. So that's a real risk. Uh, overall may be tempting to see the bill of the case of tilting at windmills or a solution looking for a problem, but it's actually worse than that.

And for a start, nothing ever gets better as a result of making it more difficult for people to vote. I think that's pretty well known. Um, but there's also the combination of that with the, uh, lack of consensus behind the bill, which shows that this bill does politicize the electoral process and even the appearance of the government having a finger on a scale should be unthinkable.

But for this bill to have made it this far, it shows that our democracy and our democratic norms, uh, already more brittle than that. And this makes the bill particularly dangerous. Um, so yeah, what's the benefit of the bill? I mean, a small delay in the final count being returned is really not a problem.

And furthermore, the lion's share of delay in forming a government tends to be the coalition negotiations. In any case, the time the final count takes isn't all that relevant. And not to mention it's far more important to have a fair election than to have a quick one. So the fa the final remaining possibility is that this is about cost and, uh, the general policy statement in the bill mentions that it will help to, uh, manage the cost of future elections.

And now this is a bad reason. In any case, we can really see just how bad it really is in this case. Uh, the largest supported cost savings I could find in relation to the proposed enrollment deadline change was an estimate of $2.4 million, uh, from the regulatory impact statement. And based on the 2023 turnout of two point, uh, eight 6 million voters, that's 84 cents per voter, I think, I think everyone would agree that you can't put a price on free and fair elections.

Uh, but this bill would put a price on free and fair elections and that price is somewhere below 84 cents per voter. So I asked the committee to recommend that this bill not pass. Uh, thanks. I think we might have brief time for questions. All. Thank you Ms. Evans. Anyone question? No. Alright.

I think, um, oh, we got one from Mr. Stevenson, I think. Yeah. Thanks. Thanks for your submission. Hopefully you can hear me okay. Like, at the end of the day, don't we have to rely on one communication method getting to people or Yes, you do, but the problem here is the automatic updates leaves a channel for the information to change without the person knowing it's changed.

They don't know. They think that their current information hasn't been altered when in fact it has, and then the, the communication doesn't reach them at all. Um, and, um, you know, like an old email address hanging around with a government department and mistakenly being sent out as a new one. Tina. Um, Tina, uh, Mr. Chair. Um, I guess we'll just get right into it. Yes. We've got five minutes. Yes. Um, so called, my name is Dan and my au is from Taiwan. Um, my great auntie immigrated to Tama Makoto with her young family in the 1980s, and we feel super lucky, um, and fortunate to have three generations here now.

Um, I strongly oppose the electoral amendment bill for many reasons, many reasons that have already been mentioned by other speakers today. Um, I just wanted to talk of what Graham was saying before, and Karina and Charlotte, um, they've put in a lot of stats and hard work into their submissions.

Um, the bill, um, as it stands proposes to institutionalize barriers and obstacles for us to exercise our voting rights. Um, I'd like to talk more about how this bill threatens accessibility and exposes much larger flaws in our system. Um, I was born in Tamaki Makoto and have spent my whole schooling life under the New Zealand education system.

I've been fortunate enough to receive higher education at the University of Auckland and a UT and through 17 years of formal education and schooling. Not once was I taught or encouraged to learn about our parliamentary system, electoral processes or constitutional rights. Um, prior to this submission, I also spoke with my friends and peers.

Many of these friends have also received high education and gone through the entire education without any kind of class or course that teaches us about civics. If we want to encourage a democratic society where everyone participates, why do we not have systems that teach us about it? If there is an existing lack of civic education and competency, why are we creating more barriers for people that are trying to engage?

I think we, um, mentioned a few of these stats earlier, but that almost a hundred thousand people enrolled to vote within the last 12 days before election day in 2023. If we have this data, we should not be entertaining a policy that would wipe these votes. If we have reports from the Attorney General demonstrating that one, this bill will further disenfranchise marginalized groups and use, and that two, the bill fundamentally violates the Bill of Rights.

We are not using this information to make informed choices. We begin to care and invest in a system when we feel that it represents us and cares for us in return. When you have a bill that blatantly suggests to remove voting rights for prisoners, you are telling that group of people already facing issues with the system that they are not important.

When we look at our youth, who will someday become the leaders for our, our grandchildren, do we wish to be the people who encourage or discourage them? Um, I had a little look on the Electoral Commission website and saw that the Orange Man says Our democracy is better with you in it. And I believe and hopes that the, you should include everyone in our generations ahead.

Thank you. Uh, My name is Ethan. I am a year 13 Wellington College student. Oh, fantastic. Very avid interest in politics. Um, how old are you? 17. 17? Yeah. Yeah. Um, and I thank this committee for the opportunity to have my first all submission to the bill today.

Um, I'm always submitting as an individual who sees the purpose, aims and intentions of this bill. I believe that holistically the electoral amendment bill is a positive step towards creating more robust, efficient, and integral electoral system that's capable of handling future population growth in New Zealand. Despite the, some aspects of this bill should be reworked to better serve the interest of all New Zealanders.

One of the largest discussion points of this bill has been talked about a lot today has been the introduction of a 13 day voter involvement deadline. Goldsmith previously told press reporters after the bill's first reading that the ability for people to enroll or update it in electoral details up to and including on election day has placed too much strain on the system, um, which was a direct contributing factor towards the final vote counts.

Taking three weeks to be released by left to mission, which was seen as one of the longest delays on record between election day and the final book return. This is a result of the rapid increase. This, this is also a result of the rapid increase in the number of people casting special votes. I believe that the move to introduce a 13 day voter enrollment deadline is a positive and effective, um, change that will help the elector to commission reduced the amount of last minute enrollment applications, therefore supporting the need

to minimize the delay in the release of final election results. Despite the efficiency this will bring, concerns have been raised about the possible disenfranchisement on more than a hundred thousand New Zealanders from voting regulatory impact statement or RIS published by the Ministry of Justice in March this year.

It's indicated over 500,000 people cast special votes in the 2023 general election. The core reason for these special votes came as a result of between 300,000 to 350,000 people either not enrolled entirely or not enrolled in the correct address or an election day.

Maori Civica and Asian groups are affected predominantly by these changes 'cause they face disadvantages in housing employment due the fact those like lower incomes or educational qualifications. This causes many people in these ethnic groups to move fiscal court justice on a more frequent basis.
